a reactive stream every layer of your app can observe independently.

associated object is released, which cancels all tasks.

abstract.text: values through delegate methods or completion handlers rather than
the rest of your app can work with a clean, cancellable stream.
Authentication state is the classic cross-cutting concern: the home
tab, the profile screen, and a UIKit sign-in controller all need to
react when the user signs in or out. Traditional approaches like singletons
a reactive stream every layer of your app can observe independently.
Search-as-you-type is a great user experience, but firing a network
request on every keystroke wastes bandwidth and hammers your server.
A debounced pipeline collapses rapid keystrokes into a single request
that fires only after the user pauses. FlowKit makes this a four-operator
Learn FlowKit by building real features. These tutorials walk through
the patterns that come up most often: search-as-you-type, shared state
across screens, wrapping callback APIs, and writing deterministic tests.
values through delegate methods or completion handlers rather than
the rest of your app can work with a clean, cancellable stream.
Real sleeps make tests slow and flaky. FlowKit ships a
virtual clock, and a suite of
helpers that let you assert on stream values

content: // SessionManager is the single source of truth for authentication state.
// Any view or component that needs to react to sign-in / sign-out gets its
// own independent observation without any manual notification plumbing.
// MutableStateFlow holds the current state and broadcasts every change
// to all active subscribers: SwiftUI views, UIKit controllers, and tests.
// Expose a read-only StateFlow so callers cannot mutate state directly.

// Test that auth errors are surfaced and recovery returns to .signedOut.
@Test("auth error surfaces as .error and recovers to .signedOut")
await state.send(.error("Invalid credentials. Please try again."))
try await tester.expectValue(.error("Invalid credentials. Please try again."))
// User can retry. State resets to .signedOut first

content: // ProfileView independently observes the same StateFlow.
// Both HomeView and ProfileView stay in sync automatically, with no
// environment objects, no NotificationCenter, and no delegates.
// A stand-in for your real auth service.
func signIn(email: String, password: String) async throws -> User
// Replace with your real AuthService in production.
func signIn(email: String, password: String) async {
let user = try await authService.signIn(email: email, password: password)
// Best-effort sign-out: clear local state regardless.
func signIn(email: String, password: String) async throws -> User {
// When the state changes anywhere in the app (after sign-in, sign-out,
// or an auth error), SwiftUI automatically re-renders this view.
// UIKit view controllers use `self.collect(_:action:)` from FlowUIKitBridge.
// The helper ties collection to `flowScope`, which is cancelled automatically
// when the view controller is deallocated, so no manual cleanup is needed.
// Observe session state changes and update the UI on the main actor.
guard let email = emailField.text, let password = passwordField.text else { return }
Task { await SessionManager.shared.signIn(email: email, password: password) }
